coredata.dc:title: Label-free tri-luminophores electrochemiluminescence sensor for microRNAs detection based on three-way DNA junction structure
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Xialing
Suo, Zhiguang
Hu, Ziheng
Zhang, Xinying
Chen, Yingying
Feng, Lingyan
coredata.dc:description: A label-free microRNAs (miRNAs) electrochemiluminescence sensor was developed based on the synergistically enhanced ECL signal using novel ECL nanomaterial assembled by tri-luminophores perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheet (g-C3N4 NS) and graphene carbon quantum dots (GQDs). The ECL intensity of our proposed nanocomposites increased significantly, by taking advantages of the synergistic promotion ECL mechanism of PTCA g-C3N4 and GQDs. Meanwhile, a three-way junction (TWJ) structure was introduced into the sensor design, whose conformation can be specifically induced in the presence of target miRNA. Thus, the formation of TWJ structure can hinder the contact between the co-reactant Na2S2O8 in the solution phase and the luminescent substrate, which produce a quenched ECL signal. Our proposed ECL biosensor provided a highly sensitive detection of miRNA-21 with a detection limit of 96 aM ranging from 100 aM to 1 nM. Furthermore, this ECL sensing system was able to successfully distinguish the target RNA from its homologous family and presented a promising response in human real samples. With excellent properties, the proposed ECL biosensor provided an efficient and ultrasensitive method for miRNAs detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA)
Graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheet (g- C3N4 NS)
Graphene carbon quantum dots (GQDs)
Synergistic electrochemiluminescence
miRNA detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs/g- C3N4 nanocomposite
The characterization of the biosensor fabrication

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( NSFC 21705106 ), the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning (No. TP2016023 ), Shanghai Sailing Program ( 17YF1406400 ), Shanghai Natural Science Foundation (Nos. 17ZR1410000 and 18ZR1415400 ), and Shanghai Rising-Star Program [ 20QA1403400 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The FT-IR spectra, (B) TGA spectra of (a)PTCA (b)GQDs (c) g-C3N4 nanosheet and (d) PTCA/GQDs/g-C3N4/Au NPs hybrid; (C) UV–vis spectra of (a) Au NPs (b)GQDs (c) g-C3N4 nanosheet (d) PTCA and (e) PTCA/GQDs/g-C3N4/Au NPs hybrid.
TEM image of (A) GQDs and (B)) g-C3N4 (C) Au NPs(D) PTCA/GQDs/g-C3N4/Au NPs.

(A) The ECL of GQDs, PTCA, PTCA/GQDs, PTCA/g-C3N4, PTCA/GQDs/g-C3N4, PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs,PTCA/GQDs/g-C3N4/Au NPs modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) with 0.1 M Na2S2O8 at the scanning potential range from 0 V to −1.8 V and (B) stability of the ECL signals of nanohybrid scanning from 0 to −1.8 V.

CV (A) and EIS (B) of stepwise-modified electrode surfaces in 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3−/4- solution containing 0.10 M KCl; (a) bare GCE, (b) GCE/PTCA composite, (c) GCE/PTCA composite/DNA (d) GCE PTCA composite/DNA/Tween20/LNA/RNA.

(A) The ECL detection of miRNA at different concentration from 100aM to 1 nM; (B) A plot of ΔECL vs. the logarithm of miRNA concentrations. (C) Continuous cyclic potential scans of the proposed ECL sensor incubated with 10pM miRNA; (D) Specificity of the ECL biosensor toward (a) blank control, (b)1 nM NC RNA, (c) 1 nM TM RNA, (d) 1 nM SM RNA, (e) 1 nM miRNA.
The layer by layer assemble process PTCA/g-C3N4/GQDs/Au NPs (A) and the fabricated process of proposed ECL biosensor for miRNA detection (B) and the synergistic ECL principle(C).
Comparison of different analytical methods for miRNA-21 detection.

head.ce:title: Label-free tri-luminophores electrochemiluminescence sensor for microRNAs detection based on three-way DNA junction structure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Genome Institute, Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, 200444 Shanghai, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Genome Institute, and Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, 200444 Shanghai, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A label-free microRNAs (miRNAs) electrochemiluminescence sensor was developed based on the synergistically enhanced ECL signal using novel ECL nanomaterial assembled by tri-luminophores perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheet (g-C3N4 NS) and graphene carbon quantum dots (GQDs). The ECL intensity of our proposed nanocomposites increased significantly, by taking advantages of the synergistic promotion ECL mechanism of PTCA g-C3N4 and GQDs. Meanwhile, a three-way junction (TWJ) structure was introduced into the sensor design, whose conformation can be specifically induced in the presence of target miRNA. Thus, the formation of TWJ structure can hinder the contact between the co-reactant Na2S2O8 in the solution phase and the luminescent substrate, which produce a quenched ECL signal. Our proposed ECL biosensor provided a highly sensitive detection of miRNA-21 with a detection limit of 96 aM ranging from 100 aM to 1 nM. Furthermore, this ECL sensing system was able to successfully distinguish the target RNA from its homologous family and presented a promising response in human real samples. With excellent properties, the proposed ECL biosensor provided an efficient and ultrasensitive method for miRNAs detection.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheet (g- C3N4 NS)

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small single-stranded endogenous noncoding RNAs of ~22 nucleotides which plays a vital role in regulating gene expression during virtually every cellular process [1]. miRNAs are potential biomarkers in early-stage cancers. It has been reported that microRNAs aberrant express in many types of cancers including breast cancer, liver cancer and lung cancer [2]. However, the abundance of miRNAs (0.01% of total RNA) is very low and the mutual interference is common in its homologous family, so it is urgent to develop ultrasensitive and selective biosensors for miRNAs. Till now, several techniques have been developed, such as northern blotting [3], quantitative real time PCR (qRT-PCR) [4], microarray analysis [5] and electrochemical techniques [6]. There are several disadvantages in these methods that are poor sensitivity, time-consuming and complexity or need expensive fluorescent labels. Thus, it is crucial to develop a more sensitive, specific and simple strategy for the accurate detection of miRNA.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) as a valuable analytical technology, has attracted wide attention in recent years because of simple operation, low background signal, and high sensitivity [7]. Till now, a series of miRNAs have been ultra-sensitively detected by constructing appropriate ECL biosensors [8,9]. In this case, the nucleotide is used to construct the biosensors for the accurate detection which is usually labelled with single luminophore decorated by nanomaterial or rely on complex catalyzed hairpin assembly (CHA) [10] or hybridization chain reaction (HCR) [11]. Therefore, it is necessary to simplify sensor design and develop an efficient and stable composite nanomaterial with multiple luminescent species.

A well-designed ECL sensor must depend on the performance of luminescent nanomaterials. As a result, the development of ECL emitting luminescent species with high efficiency has become a research hotspot [12,13]. However, traditional ECL emitters exhibit unsatisfactory biocompatibility, chemical stability and luminous efficiency. Owning to the merits of low toxicity, low-cost and good biocompatibility, nanomaterials have developed rapidly in the past decade. At the same time, in order to overcome the shortcomings of a single luminescent species, the synergy between nanomaterials is used. It is an effective strategy to obtain excellent luminous efficiency and good biocompatibility by employing multiple nanomaterials to form nanocomposites. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop more advanced ECL nanocomposites [14,15]. Xu et al. used the synergistic enhancement between PTCA and CQDs to construct an ECL sensor for the carcinoembryonic antigen detection [16]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention for its low toxicity, low-cost and good biocompatibility [17]. However, insufficient luminous efficiency limits its wide application in ECL. Graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheet (g-C3N4 NS), as a metal-free semiconductor material, has been proved to be an ideal ECL emitters for its effective, strong, and stable ECL performance [18] and it is also an ideal nanocarrier because of its large surface area. But the intrinsic poor electrical conductivity of g-C3N4 hinders its direct application in electrochemical and ECL analysis [19]. In order to improve the conductivity, gold Nanoparticles (Au NPs) have been widely studied and shown significant potential in biological due to their unique electrical conductivity and biocompatibility [20]. In order to improve the conductivity Au NPs were introduced into g-C3N4 in order to significantly increase the conductivity of the hybrid material for their unique electrical conductivity and biocompatibility which have been widely studied. Additionally, 3,4,9,10-perylene tetracarboxylic acid (PTCA), a 5-ring polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon, has a large rigid conjugate surface and abundant carboxyl modification. Furthermore, it also can enhance the ECL signal of peroxydisulfate (S2O8 2−) emission [21].

Building a good electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor requires not only efficient and stable luminescent species, skillful nucleic acid design is also critical in the construction of ECL sensors. DNA three-way junction (TWJ), a unique branched structure, has highly sensitivity and specificity [22]. Even homologous sequences with single-mismatched bases can be well recognized. In addition, LNA is a special class of nucleotide derivatives that has a methylene bridge between 2′ -o and 4′ -c on the ribose, increasing the stability of the local phosphate skeleton structure. Using LNA substitution, the thermal stability of the complex dramatically increased with each LNA, increasing the melting temperatures (Tm) by as much as 10 °C [23]. Therefore, taking advantages of high binding affinity and specificity, LNA is used as an excellent tool in biosensor designing for distinguishing target miRNA sequence.

In this report, based on nanomaterials with excellent light-emitting properties and sophisticated DNA design, we employed the tri-luminescent species nanocomposite as the signal probe and combined with the special DNA TWJ structure based on LNA to realize the ultra-sensitive and highly specific detection of miRNA. The PTCA, GQDs and g-C3N4 hybrid were facile prepared as a tri-luminophores for the development of ECL sensor as a substrate (Scheme 1A). For this nanomaterial, g-C3N4 also acts as a nanocarrier, while PTCA can easily interact with GQDs and non-covalently modify g-C3N4 through π-π interaction. PTCA and GQDs adsorbed on the surface of g-C3N4 can provide abundant -COOH groups without further damaging the conjugated system of carbon nitride. Meanwhile, Au NPs are introduced into the composites to increase the conductivity. Finally, PTCA, GQDs and g-C3N4 showed high efficiency and stable ECL emission due to the synergistic effect with Na2S2O8 as co-reactants. As shown in Scheme 1B, the ECL intensity switch is designed according to the “three-way junction” strategy, and the NH2-modified capture strand (Cp) was linked to PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs/g-C3N4 via a carbodiimide-mediated wet-chemistry approach. The Cp-PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs/g-C3N4/were incubated with the assistant probe and the target miRNA to form the TWJ structure. Therefore, the TWJ structure formed in the presence of target miRNA can block the contact between the co-reactant Na2S2O8 in the solution phase and the luminescent substrate, resulting in a significant decrease of ECL intensity.

3,4,9,10-perylene tetracarboxylic dianhydride (98% PTCDA) was purchased from Acros Organics. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were purchased from J&K Chemicals. Carbon fibers, trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7), urea (CH4N2O), sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8, ≥ 98%), 2-(N-morpholino) ethane sulfonic acid (MES) and Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China) Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), and nitric acid (HNO3) were purchased from Sino pharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. A solution of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M Na2S2O8 and 0.1 M NaCl was used as the electrolyte in ECL analysis. All HPLC-purified oligonucleotides were synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co. (Shanghai, China). The sequences of oligonucleotides are listed in Table S1. All reagents were analytical pure grade and were used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore).

ECL measurements were performed with a BPCL-Q-GP21-TGC Ultra-Weak luminescence analyser with conventional three-electrode cell consisted of a platinum wire as the auxiliary, Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as the reference electrode and the modified GCE as working electrode. Unless otherwise noted, the PMT was 1000 V and the potential scan was from 0 V to −1.8 V at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. Electrochemical measurements were investigated on an Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat instrument (Metrohm China Ltd., Switzerland). Fluorescence measurements were performed by a Fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FS-5 UK) with Xenon lamp and 10 mm quartz cells at the slits of 5 nm. UV absorbance measurements were carried out on a JASCO V-750 UV–vis spectrophotometer and circular dichroism (CD) measurements were conducted by JASCO-1500 (Japan). FT-IR characterization was measured on a Thermo Fisher Nicolet Is50 FT-IR spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were finished using a JEM-200CX transmission electron microscope. Thermogravimetry (TGA) was performed with the STA250 apparatus (Netzsch Ltd., Shanghai) at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 from 35 °C to 900 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs/g- C3N4 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: PTCA was prepared as previously described: [24] Briefly, 0.2 g PTCDA was added to 2% KOH solution. And this mixture reacted in a circular bottom flask at 80 °C for 6 h, then the HCl was used to adjust pH to 5–6 and the solution changed from yellowish green to red. Following that, the red precipitate was washed by anhydrous ethanol and deionized water over three times. After that, the solid powder was obtained by centrifugation and dried under vacuum at 60 °C.

g-C3N4 was synthesized according to previous work: [25] Typically, urea was heated to 550 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 for 2 h, then cooled to room temperature and kept for another 2 h at 500 °C at the heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Finally, g-C3N4 NS was obtained. The ultrathin g-C3N4 NS which used in our experimental was obtained by dispersing 0.3 mg of g-C3N4 powder in 1 mL of water, and ultrasound was performed for 16 h.

GQDs was prepared by the following procedure: [26] 30 mL H2SO4 and 10 mL HNO3 solution and 0.5 g carbon fibers were mixed. After 2 h ultrasonication, the mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 24 h. Then dilute the solution with deionized water and adjust pH to 8 with NaOH. Subsequently, the product was dialyzed through the molecular porous membrane tubing (MWCO: 1000, Spectrum Laboratories, Inc. US) for 3 days.

The synthetic method of Au NPs was the same as the previous literature [27]: 10 mL HAuCl4 (0.024 M) was add to 100 mL deionized water and the mixture was held at 100 °C for 1 h under vigorous stirring. Then sodium citrate solution (0.034 M) was introduced into the result solution and reacted for 15 min. Finally, the product was cooled and stored at 4 °C in dark for future use.

The preparation process of PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite is shown in Scheme 1A: firstly, 2 mg PTCA, 2 mL GQDs (0.15 mg mL−1), 1 mL Au NPs and 2 mL H2O were stirred in a round-bottomed flask for 2 days, then g-C3N4 was added and stirred for another 4 h. GQDs and carbon nitride nanosheet anchored to the PTCA through π − π interaction. Because of the strong interaction between Au and N [28], Au NPs was attached to the surface of carbon nitride nanosheet to form PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs/g-C3N4 nanohybrid.

Prior to use, the glassy carbon electrodes (GCE, ø = 3 mm, CHI) were polished with 0.3, and 0.05 μm alumina powder and then sonicated for 3 min. After 10 min sonication, 5 μL PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite was dropped on the pre-treated GCE and dried at 37 °C overnight. Then, 5 μL MES buffer (200 mM EDC and 50 mM NHS) was add to the surface incubating for 1 h in order to activate the carboxyl group. After that, 0.5 μM DNA with the terminal amine group was immediately dropped on the surface and incubated for 4 h. After being washed and dried, the surface was immersed into 1 mL 0.1% tween 20 solution for 5 min to avoid nonspecific binding. Then, the modified electrode was washed again and stored in air prior to measurement.

ECL measurements were performed in detection buffer (100 mM PBS, 100 mM NaCl pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M Na2S2O8 at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1 with a potential from 0 to −1.8 V under a PMT voltage at 1000 V. The modified electrodes were incubated with 2.5 μL assistant DNA and 2.5 μL different concentrations of miRNA for 1 h. Then ECL and CVs curves were recorded simultaneously.

The preparation of PTCA/GQDs/Au NPs/g-C3N4 had been introduced step by step in the experimental part. PTCA was a red powder. Its aqueous solution under alkaline conditions is yellow-green and exhibited very strong green fluorescence. At the same time, PTCA is a good ECL luminophore, with S2O8 2− as the co-reactant which can produce strong and stable ECL signal [24]. We used a top-down method to etch carbon fiber to synthesize GQDs [26]. Finally, a dark brown GQDs solution with yellow fluorescence was obtained, and its concentration was determined to be 0.15 mg mL−1 by a freeze dryer. On the basis of previous studies, carbon nitride was synthesized by pyrolysis using urea as raw material [25]. g-C3N4 was a white powder that can excite blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light after ultrasonic dispersion treatment. Fig. S1 illustrated the fluorescence spectroscopy of each nanomaterial. As the fluorescence of PTCA is much stronger than that of GQDs and carbon nitride, the fluorescence peak of nanocomposite was still at 480 nm and 511 nm, but the peak of carbon nitride can be observed at 511 nm, too. The composite showed an enhanced fluorescence, and its ECL intensity was significantly enhanced compared with that of the individual luminescence units (Fig. 3A).

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) was investigated to confirm the combination of different nanomaterials (Fig. 1A). For the FT-IR spectrum of PTCA (Fig. 1A, a), the peaks at 3423 cm−1 (-OH), 1640 cm−1 (C=O), 1590 cm−1 (C=C), 1440, 1390 and 1360 cm−1 (CH) were clearly observed. For g-C3N4 (Fig. 2A, c), the typical absorption peaks of the vibration of triazine ring at 810 cm−1, stretching vibration of connected units of C − N(C) − C or C − NH − C (partial condensation) at 900–1800 cm−1 and N − H at 3000–3300 cm−1 were observed. Meanwhile, there are lots of -OH and -COOH groups on the surface of GQDs according to the IR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1A, b). As seen in Fig. 1A, d, the finger-print information of PTCA, g-C3N4 and GQDs were reserved in the nanohybrid. After self-assembly on g-C3N4, although there was no change on the UV vis absorption peaks of nanocomposite around 413, 438 and 467 nm, it can be obviously found that the UV vis absorption transferred from 400 nm to 200 nm, which indicated that PTCA and GQDs participate in self-assembly in composites. The absorption peaks of Au NPs around 520 nm showed remarkably red shift after self-assembly on g-C3N4, which indicated the aggregation of Au NPs on carbon nitride (Fig. 1C). At the same time the content of PTCA in the composite was measured by TGA (Fig. 1B). The thermal decompsition at 800 °C is due to carbonization of the molecular structure. According to the mass loss shown by TGA, we can calculate the presence of PTCA and g-C3N4 in the composite material as 48.6% and 20.4%. The different kinds of spectra and TGA data showed that there are strong hydrophobic interaction and π-π stacking between PTCA and GQD/Au/g-C3N4.

The morphologies and structures of the as-prepared nanomaterials were investigated using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2A, the synthesized GQDs were uniform spherical shapes with an average diameter of 5 nm, indicating that the as-prepared GQDs were well monodispersed and uniform in size. g-C3N4 was synthesized by a simple liquid ultrasonic exfoliation according to previous work [29]. The g-C3N4 nanosheet (Fig. 2B) was observed the layered and sheet-like morphology. The TEM image of the Au NPs nanomaterial (Fig. 2C) exhibited round dots well dispersed, which was consistent with the reported literature [24]. As seen in Fig. 2D, Au NPs were uniformly distributed on the PTCA surface, suggesting that the Au NPs were successfully loaded on the surface of PTCA. For nanohybrid (Fig. 2D), the lamellar structure of g-C3N4 nanosheet and the short rod-like structure of PTCA were clearly observed, meanwhile, the Au NPs and GQDs were anchored onto the g-C3N4-PTCA hybrid.

The ECL properties of different luminescent species GQDs, and PTCA as well as hybrid nanomaterial were investigated. Fig. 3A showed the three luminescent species were able to produce ECL emission during the cyclic potential scanning between 0 and − 1.8 V in buffer with 0.1 M Na2S2O8. PTCA has demonstrated a strong ECL and by contrast, GQDs showed a very weak ECL. After Au NPs was anchored onto the nanocomposite, the ECL increased for increasing conductivity of nanocomposite by Au NPs. The strength of ECL increases significantly when PTCA self-assembled on the g-C3N4. This might be due to the fact that g-C3N4 has a large specific surface area for the immobilization of PTCA molecules, which was conducive to enhancing the self-assembly of nanomaterials and thus being able to load more light emitting units [30]. As shown in Fig. S2, we further compared the luminescence properties of different luminescent species and composite materials. Compared with the three luminescent materials alone, the ECL intensity of the composite material is increased by 112, 7.5, and 5.2 times respectively. PTCA/g-C3N4 showed a stronger ECL signal (about 5000 ECL counts) compared to a single luminescent species, and when tri-luminescent species were formed, its ECL intensity double (about 10,000 ECL counts). Compared with all the individual light-emitting species and the combination between them, the nanocomposite material we finally form has a significant enhancement. Fig. 3B exhibited the ECL intensity of the prepared nanohybrid under continuously cyclic potential scanning for 20 cycles, which indicated a good stability. And this result also demonstrated PTCA, GQDs and g-C3N4 synergistically promoted the ECL properties.

The ECL of bare GCE and PTCA/GQDs/Au/g-C3N4 modified GCE was recorded in PBS solution with Na2S2O8 (Fig. S3). No ECL signal was observed on the bare GCE in the presence of Na2S2O8 (Fig. S3 black line), indicating that the background signal from the emission of excited oxygen was excluded. In addition, there is also no ECL response for PTCA/GQDs/Au/g-C3N4 modified GCE in the absence of Na2S2O8 in Fig. S3 (red line). The red line indicated that PTCA/GQDs/Au/g-C3N4 could not produce ECL with dissolved oxygen. However, in the presence of Na2S2O8, PTCA/GQDs/Au/g-C3N4 modified GCE showed maximum ECL emission (Fig. S3, blue line), which should be due to the co-reaction of S2O8 2− and PTCA/GQDs/Au/g-C3N4.

According to the reports about the cathodic ECL of PTCA, g-C3N4 and quantum dots (QDs) [31–33], it can be confirmed that the production of cathodic ECL consists of three parts (Scheme 1C). An electron from working electrode is injected into PTCA, GQDs and g-C3N4 to produce the PTCA (PTCA•-), GQDs (GQDs•-) and g-C3N4 (g-C3N4•-), and the S2O8 2− is electro-reduced to SO4 2− and SO4•-. Subsequently, the strong oxidant SO4•- reacts with reduced species of PTCA (PTCA•-), GQDs (GQDs•-) and g-C3N4 (g-C3N4•-) to produce the excited state PTCA*, GQDs*, g-C3N4 *. Finally, PTCA*, g-C3N4 * and GQDs* decays back to the ground state PTCA, GQDs, g-C3N4, generating ECL emission.

And it was illustrated as follows:
(1)
Reduction reaction on electrode surface:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The excited state returns to the ground state:

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of the biosensor fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: The construction process of the ECL biosensor was monitored by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammograms (CV) using [Fe (CN)6]3−/4- couple as the electrochemical redox probe. As shown in Fig. 4A, the bare GCE exhibited a well-defined redox peak (curve a). Compared with the bare electrode, the redox peak currents at the PTCA composite/GCE decreased (curve b), indicating that the PTCA composite had been immobilized on the electrode surface. When capture probe immobilized onto the nanohybrid film surface via a carbodiimide-mediated wet-chemistry approach, the conductivity of the formed capture probe layer was not so good that the peak currents decreased (curve c). The peak current further decreased when Tween20 was used on the electrode due to the blocking effect of Tween20. (Fig. S4A curve d) After the immobilization of LNA, miRNA layer by layer in Fig. S4A (curve e) and Fig. 4A (curve d), the peak currents decreased gradually. This could be attributed to the fact that biological substances can hinder the diffusion of ferricyanide toward the electrode surface. The sensor assembly process was also investigated by EIS. The semicircle diameter in the impedance spectrum is equal to the electron transfer resistance (Ret), which controls the electron transfer dynamics of the electrode interface redox probe. The EIS curves and corresponding equivalent circuit were shown in Fig. 4B and its inset. As shown in Fig. 4B, the Ret of PTCA composite modified GCE (curve b) was slightly increased compared with the bare GCE (curve a), which was related to the unsatisfactory conductivity of PTCA composite. After capture probe, and miRNA were successively modified (curve c, d), the Ret increased continuously. This could be attributed to the fact that capture probe and miRNA modified onto the electrode could hinder the electron transfer between the redox probe [Fe (CN)6]3−/4- and the electrode. EIS results corresponded to CV characterization, further confirming the successful modification of the electrode.

In addition, we studied the TWJ structure in the three parts of the double helix by means of CD measurement. As shown in Fig. S5, there were significant differences between random single-stranded DNA and LNA/RNA/DNA mixtures in the CD spectrum. There were a positive band at 260–280 nm and a negative band at 245 nm, which was the typical CD peak of B-type double-chain [34], which confirmed the formation of TWJ structure.

The duration of target binding is an important experimental parameter that affects the performance of ECL sensor. Normally, the signal change value of ECL increased with the increase of the incubation time, which was attributed that more TWJ structures were introduced, so a excessively short incubation time was not conducive to the detection of low-concentration target miRNA. As shown in Fig. S6, ECL response increased significantly with the increase of incubation time and reached its maximum value at 40 min. However, when the incubation time was 60 min, the ECL reaction was relatively stable. Therefore, the incubation time was selected to be 60 min in the following experiment.

We evaluated the analytical performance of the proposed ECL strategy by investigating the ECL response in the presence of target miRNA at different concentrations (Fig. 5A). The relationship between the ECL intensity and miRNA-21 concentration was shown in Fig. 5A. With the increase of concentration of target miRNA, the strength of ECL decreased gradually, due to the formation of more and more TWJ structures on the modified electrode surface, which prevented the electrochemical reaction between Na2S2O8 and the luminescent substrate. As shown in Fig. 5B, a linear range for miRNA-21 was obtained from 100 aM to 1 nM with a detection limit of 96 aM (S/N = 3). The linear relationship could be represented as ΔI = 0.09 logC + 1.77, with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 (ΔI = %(I0 I)/I0, I0: ECL intensity in the absence of target miRNA, I: ECL intensity in the presence of target miRNA). The results indicated that the proposed ECL sensor could realize sensitive detection of miRNA. In addition, compared with other ECL sensors based on mono-luminophore modified nanomaterials shown in Table 1 , the sensor we proposed has the advantages of lower detection limit and wider detection range.

We also evaluated the stability of the ECL biosensor by scanning for 15 consecutive cycles at 10 pM of miRNA. As is shown in Fig. 5C, there was no significant change in the ECL response, which indicated a good stability of our proposed sensor. We take the maximum value of the ECL intensity corresponding to each cycle and obtain the relative standard deviation of RSD = 1.81%<2%, which also shows that our sensor has relatively good stability. The specificity of biosensor was another important indicator to evaluate the performance of sensors. In order to confirmed the specificity of the constructed biosensors, we conducted ECL experiments with miRNA, single-base mismatched chain (SM), three-base mismatched chain (TM) and non-complement chain (NC), respectively, sequences demonstrated in Table S1. As shown in Fig. 5D, the presence of SM, TM and NC chains showed extremely low signal strength even at 1 nM concentration. However, in the presence of 1 nM target miRNA-21, the ΔI intensity increased significantly, which could be clearly distinguished from other interference sequences. This result indicated the proposed biosensor has a good specificity for the determination of target miRNA.

To confirm the proposed biosensor for analysing miRNA in human serum samples, we performed ECL tests on human plasma samples collected from Renji Hospital (Shanghai, China). The serum samples were diluted 100 times with PBS buffer. As shown in Fig.S7, there were still significant signal changes in the complex serum environment. Therefore, our proposed biosensor has the potential to identify miRNA in real biological sample.

In summary, an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for specific detection of miRNA was successfully developed based on the synergistic promotion ECL mechanism of PTCA g-C3N4 and GQDs. In the presence of target miRNA, a three-way junction TWJ structure can be formed following the principle of base complementary pairing which can hinder the reaction between the co-reactant Na2S2O8 and the luminescent substrate, resulting in a significant decrease of ECL intensity. Our proposed strategy has the advantages of both wide detection range and low detection limit, which provides a surprising prospect for efficient and sensitive detection of RNA as well as clinical analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 21705106), the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning (No. TP2016023), Shanghai Sailing Program (17YF1406400), Shanghai Natural Science Foundation (Nos. 17ZR1410000 and 18ZR1415400), and Shanghai Rising-Star Program [20QA1403400].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2020.114935.
